+++ b/.kilo/plans/2026-07-11-baz-phase3-finish-and-validate.md
@@ -0,0 +1,43 @@
+# BAZ — Finish & Validate Phase 3 SEO (Next Move Plan)
+
+**Date:** 2026-07-11 · **Repo:** `baz` (`baz-marketing-site`, `origin/main`)
+**Context:** The Phase 3 plan in `1783732088378-baz-seo-offensive-phase3.md` was written as if nothing existed. A prior session has *already shipped most of it*. This plan documents what's actually done, the **real remaining gaps**, and the validation + launch sequence. Do NOT re-build what exists.
+
+## Reality check — already DONE (verified in code)
+- **T1 Service schema + form:** `serviceLd()` exists in `lib/seo.ts:129`; `app/services/[slug]/page.tsx:236` emits `serviceLd + faqLd + breadcrumbLd`; `ServiceLeadForm` wired at line 226 (`source: service_${slug}`). ✅
+- **T2 Industry schema + matrix data:** `localBusinessLd()` in `lib/seo.ts:157`; `content/locations.ts` and `lib/matrix.ts` both exist; quality gate (`publishable` + curated launch subset) implemented in `lib/matrix.ts`. ✅
+- **T3 Matrix routes:** `app/industries/[industry]/[service]`, `app/locations/[city]`, `app/locations/[city]/[industry]`, `app/locations/[city]/[industry]/[service]` all exist; each filters `publishable` + `notFound()` on non-publishable. ✅
+- **T4 Insights + full RSS:** `content/posts.ts` already has **20** posts (target met). `articleLd()` includes `author` `Person` + `sameAs` (linkedin). `app/feed.xml/route.ts` emits `<content:encoded>` CDATA with full `body`. ✅
+- **T5 Internal linking:** `CtaBanner`, `Footer`, and `ReadNext` link `/vs-others`; service pages render related `ServiceCard`s. `color-layer.css` is already imported **last** in `app/layout.tsx:23` (the T5 cosmetic item is satisfied). ✅
+- **T6 OG dynamic image:** `app/og/services/[slug]/route.tsx` exists. ✅
+
+## Real remaining gaps (the actual work)
+1. **Sitemap must include matrix routes** (plan validation #5 — currently MISSING).
+   `app/sitemap.ts` lists only static + services + cases + posts + industries. It does **not** include `/locations/[city]`, `/locations/[city]/[industry]`, `/locations/[city]/[industry]/[service]`, or `/industries/[industry]/[service]`. Build these from `buildMatrix()` (publishable only) + `cities` so Search Console can crawl the new surface.
+2. **`og:image:alt` + `og:locale` in `buildMetadata`** (`lib/seo.ts:32` — `openGraph.images` currently has no `alt`; no `og:locale` on detail pages; root layout sets `locale: en_US` but it's not propagated per-page). Add `alt` to the image object and `locale: "en_US"` to `openGraph`.
+3. **Alt-text audit on marketing-page `<Image>`s** (plan T6 item 1). Grep `app/**` for `<Image` and ensure every non-decorative image has descriptive `alt` (decorative SVGs get `aria-hidden`). Track as a checklist; fix per-page.
+
+## Validation sequence (before ship)
+1. `npm run typecheck` and `npm run lint` — clean from current tree (catches regressions from matrix/OG additions).
+2. `npm run build` — confirm generated route count ≈ launch subset (top cities × industries × services ≈ 108) + 20 posts, no build errors.
+3. `npm run sitemap` (or rely on `app/sitemap.ts` at runtime) — confirm matrix URLs present; submit to Search Console.
+4. Google Rich Results test on one of each: service, industry, city overview, leaf, insights — `Service`/`LocalBusiness`/`FAQPage`/`Breadcrumb`/`Article` present, 0 errors.
+5. RSS validator on `/feed.xml` — full `<content:encoded>` parseable.
+6. Lighthouse / PageSpeed on home, service, industry, location-leaf, insights — no regression vs Phase 1 baseline.
+
+## Rollout guardrails (from original plan — still binding)
+- **Doorway/thin-content risk:** already mitigated by `publishable` gate + `noindex` on failing combos + gated launch subset. Monitor Search Console Coverage + indexed-page count **weekly**; pause expansion if impressions/indexed drop.
+- **Truthfulness:** matrix copy must not invent local client metrics. Keep generic-but-specific local market context.
+- Expand the launch subset to the full publishable matrix only after week-1 indexing looks healthy.
+
+## Win condition (unchanged)
+Top-3 for 20+ high-intent service+industry+location queries; organic +40% in 90 days; 0 thin/doorway manual actions.
+
+## What comes AFTER Phase 3 (the next-next move — not this sprint)
+Phase 3 only makes the pages *findable*. The leverage then shifts to:
+- **Phase 4 (Speed/UX)** and **Phase 5 (Conversion)** — the matrix pages must convert, not just rank.
+- **Link earning** (original-data studies) to actually move the clusters — content exists, authority doesn't yet.
+- Decide the bigger BAZ/empire strategic move separately (out of scope here).
+
+## Recommended immediate action
+Close gaps **1–3** (sitemap matrix, og alt/locale, alt audit), run validation 1–6, then ship to production and start the weekly Search Console watch.

<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # BAZ Marketing Site — Phase 3: SEO Offensive (Implementation Plan)
2: 
3: **Target repo:** `baz` (`baz-marketing-site`, `origin/main`) · Next.js 14 App Router + TS + Tailwind + Supabase + Vercel
4: **Phase owner:** implementation-capable agent (this doc is plan-only)
5: **Prerequisite:** Phase 1 shipped in `0ba9e44`. Phase 2 (Trust & Authority) is **deferred by user** — not in scope here.
6: 
7: ## Current state (verified against code, Jul 11 2026)
8: 
9: Already done — do **not** re-do:
10: - `app/services/[slug]/page.tsx` already emits `faqLd()` + `breadcrumbLd()` (Phase 3.1 items 2 & 4 complete).
11: - `app/insights/[slug]/page.tsx` already emits `articleLd()` + `breadcrumbLd()` (Phase 3.3 item 3 complete).
12: - `app/feed.xml/route.ts` exists (emits **excerpt only**, not full content).
13: 
14: Gaps this phase closes:
15: - No `Service` schema (areaServed / offers) on service pages.
16: - `app/industries/[slug]` is single-level, has **no** JSON-LD at all, and **no** `LocalBusiness`/`ProfessionalService` schema.
17: - No location / industry×service matrix pages exist.
18: - `content/posts.ts` has **11** posts (plan assumed 5). Target = **20** → add **9**.
19: - RSS emits excerpt, not `<content:encoded>`.
20: - Some `<Image>` lack alt; no per-service OG route (`/og` dynamic route absent — only `public/og/*` static); `og:image:alt`/`og:locale` missing.
21: - `/vs-others` not linked from homepage or service footers.
22: 
23: ## Win condition
24: Top-3 rankings for 20+ high-intent service + industry + location queries; organic traffic +40% in 90 days; **0 thin/doorway manual actions** in Search Console.
25: 
26: ## Resolved decisions
27: 1. **Phase 3.2 scope = programmatic matrix** (user choice), with the guardrails in Risks below.
28: 2. **City set** (curated, editable in `content/locations.ts`): MENA `[Dubai, Riyadh, Abu Dhabi, Cairo, Casablanca]`, EU `[London, Paris, Berlin, Amsterdam]`, NA `[New York, Austin, Toronto]` → 13 cities. Matrix = 6 industries × 18 services × 13 cities.
29: 3. **Service `offers`**: generic "Contact for quote" `Offer` (`priceCurrency: "USD"`, no fixed price) — no new per-service price data required.
30: 4. **Insights +9 articles**: drafted using the `marketing-docs/` references + existing editorial voice; each must ship a real `body` (needed for RSS full content and to avoid thin pages).
31: 
32: ## Tasks (ordered)
33: 
34: ### T1 — Service schema + tailored form (Phase 3.1)
35: - `lib/seo.ts`: add `serviceLd(service)` → `@type: Service` with `name`, `description`, `provider` (Organization), `serviceType`, `areaServed` (from `site.areasServed`), `url`, `offers` (contact-for-quote). Add `site.areasServed = ["MENA","EU","US"]` to `lib/site.ts`; reuse it in `professionalServiceLd()`.
36: - `app/services/[slug]/page.tsx`: append `serviceLd(service)` to the existing JSON-LD array.
37: - Add a tailored inline mini-form ("Start a {service.name} project": name / email / goal) posting to `/api/leads` with `source: "service_${slug}"` and `service` field (reuse existing lead schema; do **not** invent a new endpoint).
38: - Validate with Google Rich Results test (Service + FAQPage + Breadcrumb, no errors).
39: 
40: ### T2 — Industry schema + matrix data (Phase 3.2 base)
41: - `app/industries/[slug]/page.tsx`: add `LocalBusiness`/`ProfessionalService` JSON-LD with `areaServed` (global regions) + `breadcrumbLd` (visual `Breadcrumb` already renders; JSON-LD is missing).
42: - `content/locations.ts` (new) + `City` type in `@/types`: `{ slug, name, country, region, locale, marketBlurb, localProof: string[] }` for the 13 cities above.
43: - `lib/matrix.ts` (new): `buildMatrix()` returns all `(industry, service, city)` combos, each with **uniquely composed** copy (H1, intro, challenges/outcomes adapted from industry + service process/deliverables + city `marketBlurb`/`localProof`) and a `publishable` boolean from a **quality gate**: ≥2 unique local data points AND body ≥ ~400 non-boilerplate words. Combos failing the gate are excluded from `generateStaticParams` and forced `noindex`.
44: 
45: ### T3 — Matrix routes (Phase 3.2)
46: Add route segments (reuse existing components: `ServiceHero`-lite, challenges/outcomes lists, `ServiceCard`, `CaseStudyCard`, `CtaBanner`, `Breadcrumb`):
47: - `app/industries/[industry]/[service]/page.tsx` — industry × service.
48: - `app/locations/[city]/page.tsx` — city overview; `LocalBusiness` schema `areaServed: city`.
49: - `app/locations/[city]/[industry]/page.tsx` — city × industry.
50: - `app/locations/[city]/[industry]/[service]/page.tsx` — full leaf; `ProfessionalService` schema `areaServed: [city, region]`, `offers` contact-for-quote.
51: - Each: `generateStaticParams` from `publishable` combos only; `export const revalidate = 86400`; `export const dynamicParams = true`; `buildMetadata` with canonical + city/industry in title; JSON-LD block.
52: - **Launch subset**: gate the first build to a curated launch set (e.g. top 3 cities × 6 industries × top 6 services ≈ 108 pages), then expand as copy quality is confirmed. Keep the rest `noindex` until reviewed.
53: 
54: ### T4 — Insights expansion + full RSS (Phase 3.3)
55: - `content/posts.ts`: add 9 articles → 20. Mix: 3 comparison pieces ("BAZ vs {Competitor}") linked from `/vs-others`; 5 "how-we-do-it" methodology posts linked from `/methodology`; 3 local-perspective (MENA/EU/US) posts; fill remainder to 9. Every new post needs a real `body`.
56: - `app/insights/[slug]/page.tsx`: already has `articleLd` + `breadcrumbLd`; add `author` `Person` + `sameAs` to satisfy E-E-A-T.
57: - `app/feed.xml/route.ts`: add `xmlns:content="http://purl.org/rss/1.0/modules/content/"` and a `<content:encoded>` CDATA containing the full `body` (Phase 3.3 item 4). Keep `cache-control` header.
58: 
59: ### T5 — Internal linking & crawl budget (Phase 3.4)
60: - Add `RelatedArticles` + `RelatedServices` modules on service / industry / insights detail pages (pull from `posts`/`services` by category).
61: - Link `/vs-others` from the homepage hero/footer **and** every service page footer (`CtaBanner` variant or a footer link).
62: - Tidy the `color-layer.css` import-order comment in `app/layout.tsx` (cosmetic; ensures `color-layer.css` is last per `DESIGN-SYSTEM.md`).
63: 
64: ### T6 — Images & OG (Phase 3.5)
65: - Audit every `<Image>` on marketing pages for alt; add descriptive `alt` (or `aria-hidden` for decorative SVGs).
66: - Add dynamic OG: `app/og/services/[slug]/route.tsx` using `next/og` `ImageResponse` (service name + tagline + brand wordmark). Wire `buildMetadata` `image` to `/og/services/${slug}` for service pages; extend similarly for industries/locations if time permits.
67: - Add `og:image:alt` and `og:locale` to `buildMetadata` (OG/Twitter blocks).
68: 
69: ## Affected boundaries
70: - New files: `content/locations.ts`, `lib/matrix.ts`, `app/og/services/[slug]/route.tsx`, 4 matrix route files, `RelatedArticles`/`RelatedServices` components.
71: - Modified: `lib/seo.ts`, `lib/site.ts`, `app/services/[slug]/page.tsx`, `app/industries/[slug]/page.tsx`, `app/insights/[slug]/page.tsx`, `content/posts.ts`, `app/feed.xml/route.ts`, `@/types`, `app/layout.tsx`.
72: - **Design-system rule:** no hardcoded hex; use semantic tokens (`text-brand`, `bg-background`, `bg-accent`). Square corners; `rounded-full` only for pills.
73: 
74: ## Data flow (matrix page)
75: `content/industries.ts` + `content/services.ts` + `content/locations.ts` → `lib/matrix.buildMatrix()` composes unique copy + `publishable` flag → route `generateStaticParams` filters `publishable` → page renders components + JSON-LD (`ProfessionalService`/`LocalBusiness` + `Breadcrumb`). Thin combos → excluded + `noindex`.
76: 
77: ## Failure modes & rollout
78: - **Doorway / thin-content penalty (highest risk):** mitigate via quality gate, `noindex` on failing combos, genuinely unique local copy (no fabricated stats — tie to Phase 2 "no placeholders" principle), strong internal linking, and a **gated launch** (subset first). Monitor Search Console Coverage + indexed-page count weekly; pause expansion if impressions/indexed drop.
79: - **Build size/time:** ~1.4k potential pages → bound with `revalidate` (ISR) + `dynamicParams = true`; launch subset keeps initial build small.
80: - **Truthfulness:** matrix copy must not invent local client metrics. Use generic-but-specific local market context; mark any illustrative figure explicitly.
81: - **RSS breakage:** validate feed after adding `<content:encoded>` (well-formed XML, CDATA-escaped).
82: 
83: ## Validation plan
84: 1. Google Rich Results / schema validator on service, industry, city, and leaf pages — `Service`/`LocalBusiness`/`FAQPage`/`Breadcrumb`/`Article` present, 0 errors.
85: 2. `npm run build` clean from a clean clone; confirm generated route count matches the launch subset.
86: 3. Lighthouse / PageSpeed Insights on 5 reps (home, service, industry, location leaf, insights) — no regression vs Phase 1.
87: 4. RSS validator on `/feed.xml` — full `<content:encoded>` present and parseable.
88: 5. Submit updated `sitemap.xml` (must include new matrix routes) to Search Console; monitor indexing + manual actions.
89: 6. Rank tracker snapshot at launch; weekly organic KW delta + indexed-page count.
90: 
91: ## Out of scope
92: - Phase 2 (Trust & Authority), Phase 4 (Speed/UX), Phase 5 (Conversion), Phase 6 (Competitive Intel).
93: - Signed case studies / real testimonials (Phase 2 dependency).
94: 
95: ## Open questions (proposed defaults — confirm or override)
96: - Final city list (proposed 13 above) — editable in `content/locations.ts`.
97: - Launch subset size (proposed top-3 cities × 6 industries × top-6 services ≈ 108) vs. full matrix on day one.
98: - `offers` representation (proposed contact-for-quote; switch to real prices later if `content/pricing.ts` gains per-service prices).

(End of file - total 98 lines)
</content>

<path>/home/uzer/baz/lib/seo.ts</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: import type { Metadata } from "next";
2: import { site } from "./site";
3: 
4: type SeoInput = {
5:   title?: string;
6:   description?: string;
7:   path?: string;
8:   image?: string;
9:   type?: "website" | "article";
10:   publishedTime?: string;
11:   noindex?: boolean;
12: };
13: 
14: /**
15:  * Builds a Next.js Metadata object with consistent Open Graph, Twitter,
16:  * canonical URL, and theme color. Pass a path (without site origin) and
17:  * we'll construct the canonical automatically.
18:  */
19: export function buildMetadata({
20:   title,
21:   description = site.description,
22:   path = "/",
23:   image = "/og/default.png",
24:   type = "website",
25:   publishedTime,
26:   noindex = false,
27: }: SeoInput = {}): Metadata {
28:   const fullTitle = title ? `${title} · ${site.shortName}` : `${site.name} — ${site.tagline}`;
29:   const url = new URL(path, site.url).toString();
30:   const img = new URL(image, site.url).toString();
31: 
32:   return {
33:     title: fullTitle,
34:     description,
35:     metadataBase: new URL(site.url),
36:     alternates: { canonical: url },
37:     openGraph: {
38:       type,
39:       siteName: site.name,
40:       title: fullTitle,
41:       description,
42:       url,
43:       images: [{ url: img, width: 1200, height: 630, alt: fullTitle }],
44:       ...(publishedTime ? { publishedTime } : {}),
45:     },
46:     twitter: {
47:       card: "summary_large_image",
48:       title: fullTitle,
49:       description,
50:       images: [img],
51:       site: "@bazagency",
52:       creator: "@bazagency",
53:     },
54:     robots: noindex
55:       ? { index: false, follow: false }
56:       : { index: true, follow: true, googleBot: { index: true, follow: true, "max-snippet": -1 } },
57:   };
58: }
59: 
60: /**
61:  * Next.js 14 requires themeColor in a separate `viewport` export.
62:  * Apply alongside `metadata` in each page or layout.
63:  */
64: export const viewport = {
65:   themeColor: "#020617", // Midnight Terminal — mobile chrome matches bg
66:   width: "device-width",
67:   initialScale: 1,
68:   colorScheme: "dark",
69: };
70: 
71: type JsonLd = Record<string, any> | Record<string, any>[];
72: 
73: /**
74:  * Renders JSON-LD structured data. Use inside a server component.
75:  * Pass a single object or an array of @graph nodes.
76:  */
77: export function jsonLd(data: JsonLd) {
78:   return {
79:     __html: JSON.stringify(data).replace(/</g, "\\u003c"),
80:   };
81: }
82: 
83: export function organizationLd() {
84:   return {
85:     "@context": "https://schema.org",
86:     "@type": "Organization",
87:     name: site.name,
88:     url: site.url,
89:     logo: new URL("/og/logo.svg", site.url).toString(),
90:     email: site.email,
91:     sameAs: Object.values(site.social),
92:   };
93: }
94: 
95: export function websiteLd() {
96:   return {
97:     "@context": "https://schema.org",
98:     "@type": "WebSite",
99:     name: site.name,
100:     url: site.url,
101:     potentialAction: {
102:       "@type": "SearchAction",
103:       target: `${site.url}/insights?q={search_term_string}`,
104:       "query-input": "required name=search_term_string",
105:     },
106:   };
107: }
108: 
109: export function professionalServiceLd() {
110:   return {
111:     "@context": "https://schema.org",
112:     "@type": "ProfessionalService",
113:     name: site.name,
114:     url: site.url,
115:     image: new URL("/og/default.png", site.url).toString(),
116:     description: site.description,
117:     areaServed: site.areasServed,
118:     priceRange: "$$$",
119:     email: site.email,
120:     telephone: site.phone,
121:   };
122: }
123: 
124: /**
125:  * Schema.org Service block for a single service detail page.
126:  * `areaServed` comes from the global regions config; `offers` is a
127:  * contact-for-quote Offer (no fixed price — engagements are scoped per client).
128:  */
129: export function serviceLd(service: {
130:   name: string;
131:   description: string;
132:   slug: string;
133:   tagline?: string;
134: }) {
135:   return {
136:     "@context": "https://schema.org",
137:     "@type": "Service",
138:     name: service.name,
139:     description: service.description,
140:     serviceType: service.tagline,
141:     url: new URL(`/services/${service.slug}`, site.url).toString(),
142:     provider: { "@type": "Organization", name: site.name, url: site.url },
143:     areaServed: site.areasServed,
144:     offers: {
145:       "@type": "Offer",
146:       priceCurrency: "USD",
147:       description: "Custom engagement — contact for a tailored quote.",
148:     },
149:   };
150: }
151: 
152: /**
153:  * LocalBusiness / ProfessionalService block for industry and location pages.
154:  * `areaServed` defaults to the global regions but can be overridden with a
155:  * specific city + region for location-leaf pages.
156:  */
157: export function localBusinessLd(opts: {
158:   name: string;
159:   description: string;
160:   url: string;
161:   areaServed?: string[];
162: }) {
163:   return {
164:     "@context": "https://schema.org",
165:     "@type": "ProfessionalService",
166:     name: opts.name,
167:     description: opts.description,
168:     url: new URL(opts.url, site.url).toString(),
169:     image: new URL("/og/default.png", site.url).toString(),
170:     areaServed: opts.areaServed ?? site.areasServed,
171:     priceRange: "$$$",
172:     email: site.email,
173:     telephone: site.phone,
174:   };
175: }
176: 
177: export function faqLd(faqs: { q: string; a: string }[]) {
178:   return {
179:     "@context": "https://schema.org",
180:     "@type": "FAQPage",
181:     mainEntity: faqs.map(({ q, a }) => ({
182:       "@type": "Question",
183:       name: q,
184:       acceptedAnswer: { "@type": "Answer", text: a },
185:     })),
186:   };
187: }
188: 
189: export function breadcrumbLd(items: { name: string; url: string }[]) {
190:   return {
191:     "@context": "https://schema.org",
192:     "@type": "BreadcrumbList",
193:     itemListElement: items.map((item, i) => ({
194:       "@type": "ListItem",
195:       position: i + 1,
196:       name: item.name,
197:       item: new URL(item.url, site.url).toString(),
198:     })),
199:   };
200: }
201: 
202: export function articleLd(post: {
203:   title: string;
204:   excerpt: string;
205:   slug: string;
206:   author: string;
207:   publishedAt: string;
208: }) {
209:   return {
210:     "@context": "https://schema.org",
211:     "@type": "Article",
212:     headline: post.title,
213:     description: post.excerpt,
214:     url: new URL(`/insights/${post.slug}`, site.url).toString(),
215:     datePublished: post.publishedAt,
216:     author: { "@type": "Person", name: post.author, sameAs: site.social.linkedin },
217:     publisher: {
218:       "@type": "Organization",
219:       name: site.name,
220:       logo: { "@type": "ImageObject", url: new URL("/og/logo.svg", site.url).toString() },
221:     },
222:   };
223: }

# BAZventures — Next.js + Supabase + Vercel

**Brand:** BAZventures · **Product:** the Hub · **Founder:** Brahim ZERBOUT  
**Design system source of truth:** `DESIGN-SYSTEM.md` (read it first).

## Project Setup
- GitHub repo: `baz-marketing-site` (remote: `origin`)
- Next.js 14 App Router + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS
- Supabase (PostgreSQL, Auth, Realtime, Storage, Edge Functions)
- Vercel (Analytics, Speed Insights, OG Images, Cron, Edge Runtime)
- CI/CD via GitHub Actions (test → deploy preview → deploy production)

## Design — Black & White Color System
- **B&W by default.** Color is a single expert-editable layer: `app/color-layer.css` (imported **last** in `app/layout.tsx` → always wins). Set `--seed-hue`/`--seed-sat` there to recolor the whole site (e.g. cyan `187/90%`, gold `42/85%`). One file, two numbers.
- **Dark mode only** — `ThemeProvider` forces `dark` (`forcedTheme="dark"`, `enableSystem={false}`). Background/text are seed-driven neutrals (currently greyscale).
- **4-layer CSS stack:** `globals.css` → `aether-theme.css` → `aether-monochrome.css` → `color-layer.css` (last wins). Full map in `DESIGN-SYSTEM.md`.
- **Fonts:** Outfit (display), Poppins (body), JetBrains Mono (code/metrics).
- **Square corners everywhere** (`* { border-radius: 0 }`); `rounded-full` only for pills/badges.
- **Tokens, not hexes:** never hardcode color in components — use semantic Tailwind classes (`text-foreground`, `bg-background`, `text-brand`, `bg-accent`, `border-border`) → tokens → `color-layer.css`.

## Brand Assets (B&W)
- **Favicon:** `public/favicon.svg` — B&W mark (dark squircle, light "B").
- **Logo (live):** a **text wordmark** rendered in `components/layout/Header.tsx` + `Footer.tsx` — a `bg-foreground` "B" square + "BAZventures" in Outfit. B&W, token-driven, auto-recolors via `--brand` when the seed is set.
- **Legacy logo SVGs** in `public/logo/` (`baz-mark*.svg`, `baz-wordmark*.svg`) are retained for the `/brandbook` reference page but are **not** used by the live header/footer.
- **OG images:** `/og`, `/stance-og`, `/case-studies-og`, `/methodology-og` — neutral palette, "BAZventures" wordmark.
- **No gold/accent color** anywhere on the public site until the expert sets the seed in `color-layer.css`.

## Key Scripts
- `npm run dev` — local dev (port 3000)
- `npm run build` — production build
- `npm test` — Playwright E2E tests
- `npm run supabase:types` — regenerate TS types from Supabase
- `npm run supabase:migrate` — push migrations to Supabase
- `npm run deploy` — deploy to Vercel

## Supabase
- Project: `uyqgmdrzyapbbvmaumvk.supabase.co`
- Migrations: `supabase/migrations/`
- Edge Functions: `supabase/functions/`
- Types: `lib/database.types.ts`
- Client: `lib/supabase-client.ts` (SSR + Admin + Anonymous)
- Realtime: `lib/realtime.ts` (client-side hooks)

## Vercel
- Project: `baz-marketing-site-y6bq` (team: brahimamirzerbout-9700s-projects)
- Analytics + Speed Insights in layout
- Dynamic OG images at `/og`
- Cron jobs: sitemap (daily), audit (weekly)
- Edge runtime: `/api/services`, `/api/health`

## CI/CD (GitHub Actions)
- `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`
- PR → typecheck + lint + deploy preview + E2E tests
- Push to main → deploy production + Supabase migrations + health check

</system-reminder>

1: import type { Post } from "@/types";

5:     slug: "compounding-seo",
6:     title: "Compounding SEO: the only channel that ages like wine",
7:     excerpt:
8:       "Why SEO is the only growth channel where yesterday's work still pays off next year — and how to build one that compounds.",
9:     body: `Most channels are renting attention. SEO is owning it.
10: 
11: When you stop publishing, paid traffic stops. When you stop posting, social traffic decays. But SEO compounds: every article you ship, every link you earn, every technical fix you make stays in the index and keeps producing traffic 24/7/365.
12: 
13: The math is simple. A piece ranking top-3 for a 5,000/mo query produces 60,000 sessions a year — for free. A piece ranking for ten such queries produces 600,000 sessions a year. After 24 months of compounding, that single content investment is outperforming most paid budgets.
14: 
15: But compounding only happens when three things are right: technical SEO, topical authority, and link earning.
16: 
17: Technical SEO is the foundation. Crawlability, indexation, Core Web Vitals, schema, internal linking. Get this wrong and nothing else matters.
18: 
19: Topical authority is the strategy. A site that has 200 high-quality pages on "B2B SaaS analytics" outranks a site with 2,000 scattered pages across random topics.
20: 
21: Link earning is the multiplier. Authority compounds faster than content quality, but only when the content deserves it.
22: 
23: The mistake most agencies make is treating SEO as a content mill. The mistake most in-house teams make is treating SEO as a checklist. The truth is editorial: SEO is a publishing business that happens to rank.`,
24:     category: "seo",
25:     author: "Brahim ZERBOUT",
26:     publishedAt: "2026-06-18",

31:     title: "Paid attribution after iOS: a survival guide",
32:     excerpt:
33:       "Server-side tracking, conversion APIs, and the new rules of measuring paid media without losing your mind.",
34:     body: `The old attribution model is dead. Browser-side pixels lost 30–60% of conversion signal on iOS. Cookies are being deprecated. Marketers pretending otherwise are flying blind.
35: 
36: Server-side tracking is the new floor, not the ceiling. Google Tag Manager's server container, Meta\'s Conversion API, TikTok\'s Events API — these give you deterministic signal that survives iOS and Chrome's cookie phase-out.
37: 
38: The pattern:
39: 
40: 1. Fire conversion events from your backend to each platform's API.
41: 2. Send the same event_id to GA4 via server GTM and to ad platforms.
42: 3. Deduplicate in-platform using event_id.
43: 4. Send hashed PII (email, phone) for advanced matching.
44: 
45: This gives you 95%+ signal recovery. Most teams stop there. The teams that win go further: they build a single source of truth for revenue (a warehouse or even a SQLite table) and reconcile every platform's numbers against it weekly.
46: 
47: The platforms lie. Their numbers are always 5–20% optimistic. The warehouse is the only place you see reality.
48: 
49: Attribution is the moat. Most agencies can't build it. Most in-house teams don't have time. The few teams who can are the ones whose dashboards execs actually open.`,
50:     category: "paid",
51:     author: "Brahim ZERBOUT",
52:     publishedAt: "2026-06-11",
53:     readingMin: 7,
54:   },
55:   {
56:     slug: "winning-the-ai-overview",
57:     title: "How to win the AI Overview",
58:     excerpt:
59:       "AI Overviews and LLM answers are the new top of the SERP. Here is how to be cited, not just ranked.",
60:     body: `If your SEO strategy hasn't changed in 12 months, you are already losing traffic to AI Overviews.
61: 
62: The click-through rate on queries with AI Overviews is down 20–40% year-over-year. The page that gets cited in the AI Overview gets the rest — and the brand impression that doesn't show up in GA4.
63: 
64: Three pillars of winning the new SERP:
65: 
66: 1. Entity-first content. LLMs and Google's AI Overviews work on entities, not keywords. Get your entity (brand, product, founder) on Wikidata, in schema, and in citation sources. The model needs to know you exist as a thing.
67: 
68: 2. Citation-worthy structure. The pages that get cited have clear claims, named sources, and quotable sentences. Not 1,500-word fluffy intros. Specificity wins.
69: 
70: 3. Author authority. Author bios with credentials, bylines across publications, and outbound links to authoritative sources. Models weight author signals heavily.
71: 
72: The teams that crack this early will own the next decade of search. The teams that don't will watch their traffic decay in slow motion.`,
73:     category: "ai",
74:     author: "Brahim ZERBOUT",
75:     publishedAt: "2026-06-04",
76:     readingMin: 5,
77:   },
78:   {
79:     slug: "cro-without-slop",
80:     title: "CRO without the slop",
81:     excerpt:
82:       "Conversion rate optimization is the most abused word in growth. Here is what good CRO actually looks like.",
83:     body: `Most "CRO" is a junior changing button colors and A/B testing copy that doesn't matter.
84: 
85: Real CRO starts with a funnel. Where are users dropping? Where is intent highest but conversion lowest? What does the data say vs. what does the team feel?
86: 
87: A rigorous CRO program runs 5–10 tests a month, each with a clear hypothesis tied to a specific metric. It uses a stats engine (Evan Miller's calculator, or built-in tools) and doesn't call winners until they hit 95% confidence.
88: 
89: It also tests the right things. Hero copy, pricing structure, social proof placement, signup flow friction — these move the needle. Color contrast on the CTA doesn't.
90: 
91: The mistake most agencies make is running tests that are easy to ship, not tests that move the metric. The mistake most in-house teams make is running too few tests to learn anything.
92: 
93: BAZ runs 60+ tests per quarter per client. Most teams run 4. That's the gap.`,
94:     category: "analytics",
95:     author: "Brahim ZERBOUT",

97:     readingMin: 6,

99:   {

101:     title: "Editorial as a moat",
102:     excerpt: "Why the best growth teams are publishers first and marketers second.",
103:     body: `The best B2B and DTC brands of the next decade will be publishers. Not because content is cheap, but because it compounds in a way no other channel does.
104: 
105: A paid campaign stops when the budget stops. An email list decays if you stop emailing. A podcast ages.
106: 
107: An article published in 2022 is still ranking, still converting, still cited. The work has been done. The traffic is free.
108: 
109: The teams winning at this treat editorial as a product, not a campaign. They have editors, writers, a calendar, a CMS workflow, and a measurement framework. They publish 8–24 pieces a month with the same rigor a media company applies to its flagship publication.
110: 
111: The mistake most agencies make is treating content as a service line, not a strategy. The mistake most brands make is treating it as an afterthought.
112: 
113: BAZ runs editorial as an operating discipline, not a deliverable. That is the moat.`,
114:     category: "content",

116:     publishedAt: "2026-05-21",
117:     readingMin: 5,
118:   },

120:     slug: "the-90-day-plan",
121:     title: "The 90-day growth plan",
122:     excerpt: "Why BAZ plans in 90 days, not 12 months — and what that means for compounding.",
123:     body: `A 12-month plan is a deck. A 90-day plan is a contract.
124: 
125: Most agencies pitch annual roadmaps because they obscure accountability. Three months is short enough that the work has to ship, the metrics have to move, and the strategy has to be honest about assumptions.
126: 
127: BAZ plans in 90-day cycles for three reasons:
128: 
129: 1. Honesty. A 12-month plan is a wish list. A 90-day plan has named owners, dated milestones, and metrics that have to move.
130: 
131: 2. Learning. Three months is enough to learn whether a hypothesis was right. Twelve months is enough to bury it.
132: 
133: 3. Compounding. Each 90-day cycle builds on the last. By month 12, you've shipped four iterations — not one giant plan that may or may not work.
134: 
135: The 90-day structure:
136: - Days 1–14: Audit and strategy.
137: - Days 15–30: Foundations (tracking, content, design system).
138: - Days 31–75: Execution (campaigns, content, tests).
139: - Days 76–90: Review and replan.
140: 
141: Most of our clients renew after their first 90 days. The plan works. So does the rhythm.`,

144:     publishedAt: "2026-05-14",

153:       "The conventional agency pyramid sells at the top and delivers at the bottom. We rejected that, and paid for it.",
154:     body: `When we started BAZ, the conventional wisdom was clear: build a junior-heavy pyramid, sell at the top, deliver at the bottom. We rejected that.

156: In our model, the person who pitched the work is the person who ships it. No junior BA synthesizing the call. No account manager translating between strategy and execution. The senior crafts the brief, ships the work, and answers the email.

167:     excerpt:
168:       "Most agencies treat distribution as an afterthought. We treat it as the first constraint.",
169:     body: `Most agencies treat distribution as an afterthought — something the media team handles once the creative is "done." We think that's wrong.

172: 
173: In our process, distribution is part of the brief from day one. Before we write a line of copy, we know the platforms, the formats, the algorithms, the audiences, the cadence. The creative comes after.`,

181:     title: "Original data is the only SEO moat that lasts",

183:       "After three algorithm updates targeting helpful content, the winning pattern is clear: ship data nobody else has.",
184:     body: `After three algorithm updates targeting "helpful content," the pattern is clear: thin, derivative content gets buried. The strategy that works: original data.
185: 
186: Five formats that work for us right now:

189: 2. **Internal data, anonymized.** "We analyzed 12M ad impressions — here's what worked."
190: 3. **Industry tooling.** Free calculators, graders, generators. People link to tools.
191: 4. **Expert roundups with original synthesis.** Not "10 CEOs say X." Instead: "We interviewed 30 CEOs, here's the 4-part pattern."
192: 5. **Live dashboards.** Public, always-updated data. Build once, link forever.`,
193:     category: "seo",

195:     publishedAt: "2026-04-22",

197:   },

199:     slug: "brand-is-a-verb",

202:       "A brand isn't a logo on a balance sheet. It's the pattern of behavior a company exhibits across every touchpoint.",
203:     body: `Most companies treat "brand" as an asset on a balance sheet — a logo, a color, a font. It's something they own.
204: 
205: That framing is broken. A brand is the pattern of behavior a company exhibits across every touchpoint. The way the support rep answers. The way the product breaks. The way the CEO tweets. None of that is on a balance sheet, all of it is the brand.

213:   {

218:     body: `Our average engagement is eight weeks. Our longest retainer renewal is six years. Both are downstream of one operating principle: ship fast, iterate faster.
219: 
220: The OS that makes this possible:
221: 
222: - **No pitches longer than two weeks.** Anything more complex gets scoped down or rejected.
223: - **Senior team on every call.** No translation layer, no re-briefing.
224: - **Async-first.** Decisions in writing. Meetings are exceptions, not defaults.
225: - **Public roadmap per client.** They see what's shipping next, this week.
226: - **Weekly demos, not weekly status.** "Look at what shipped" beats "here's what we're doing."
227: 
228: We gave up: predictability, scope-creep tolerance, and the ability to say yes to every brief. We gained: speed, trust, and a renewal rate that compounds.`,

237:     excerpt:
238:       "Why a 4-person senior team out-executes a 40-person agency — and what it costs you to find out the hard way.",
239:     body: `Traditional agencies scale by headcount. More accounts means more juniors, more hand-offs, and more time spent re-briefing than doing. The senior people you met in the pitch are not the people shipping your work.
240: 
241: BAZventures inverts that. The partner you meet is the partner who ships. No translation layer, no account-manager telephone game, no junior learning on your budget.
242: 
243: The trade-off is capacity. We take fewer engagements and we tell prospects honestly when we're not the right fit. What you get in return is speed and judgment that a larger roster can't match — and a renewal rate that compounds because the work actually moves pipeline.
244: 
245: If you need a logo refresh and a billboard, we're the wrong call. If you need a forecast you can take to a board, we're built for exactly that.`,

253:     title: "BAZventures vs an in-house growth team",
254:     excerpt:
255:       "When it's cheaper to rent senior judgment than to hire it — and the hidden cost of building the team too early.",
256:     body: `Hiring a full growth team is a $600K+ annual commitment before a single campaign runs. For most companies between $2M and $50M ARR, that team is also underutilized: you don't need five specialists every week, you need one senior operator who knows which specialist to pull in.
257: 
258: An in-house team wins on context and continuity. It loses on breadth of repetition — a senior agency has shipped the same playbook across 60+ brands and sees the pattern you can't from inside one company.
259: 
260: The honest answer is usually both, in sequence: rent the judgment first to find the model, then hire the team to run it. Hiring before the model exists just scales the confusion.`,
261:     category: "strategy",
262:     author: "Brahim ZERBOUT",
263:     publishedAt: "2026-05-16",
264:     readingMin: 4,
265:   },
266:   {
267:     slug: "bazventures-vs-hubspot-only",
268:     title: "BAZventures vs a HubSpot-only stack",
269:     excerpt:
270:       "A CRM is a database, not a growth strategy. What changes when the software stops being the hero.",
271:     body: `HubSpot is a competent system of record. The mistake is treating the subscription as the strategy. A clean CRM with no one interpreting the funnel is just an expensive contact list.
272: 
273: We use the Hub as plumbing and put senior judgment on top: which stage is leaking, what offer resets the curve, where attribution lies. The platform reports; it doesn't decide.
274: 
275: If your "marketing" is mostly configuring workflows and waiting for the tool to convert people, you've bought software instead of growth. The fix isn't a better CRM — it's a human who knows what the numbers mean and what to do Monday.`,
276:     category: "analytics",
277:     author: "Brahim ZERBOUT",
278:     publishedAt: "2026-05-23",
279:     readingMin: 3,
280:   },
281:   {
282:     slug: "the-90-day-growth-sprint",
283:     title: "The 90-day growth sprint: how we run it",
284:     excerpt:
285:       "A senior-led operating rhythm that turns a vague growth goal into a forecast you can defend to a board.",
286:     body: `Every engagement starts the same way: a growth audit, not a deck. We map the funnel, the unit economics, and the channel mix, then model LTV/CAC by source so we know where payback actually happens.
287: 
288: Weeks 1–2 are diagnosis and a 90-day roadmap with owners, budgets, and exit criteria. Weeks 3–10 ship the highest-leverage plays first — usually fixing measurement and one acquisition channel. Weeks 11–12 consolidate what worked into a repeatable system and hand over the dashboard.
289: 
290: The discipline is the point. Monthly embedded reviews with a senior partner keep it honest. If a play isn't moving pipeline by week six, we kill it and reallocate — no sunk-cost loyalty to a tactic.`,
291:     category: "strategy",
292:     author: "Brahim ZERBOUT",
293:     publishedAt: "2026-05-30",
294:     readingMin: 4,
295:   },
296:   {
297:     slug: "our-content-engine",
298:     title: "Our content engine: from brief to ranked",
299:     excerpt:
300:       "Editorial, not a content mill. The repeatable system behind pages that compound instead of decay.",
301:     body: `Most content programs fail because they're checklist-driven: publish X posts, hope for traffic. Ours is editorial — we treat SEO as a publishing business that happens to rank.
302: 
303: It starts with a brief tied to a funnel stage and a cluster, not a keyword. We write for a specific reader with a specific objection, then engineer the page for crawlability, internal links, and entity clarity so machines and humans both get it.
304: 
305: Each piece earns its place by linking into a topic cluster and picking up internal references from newer posts. That's what compounds: not volume, but a graph of pages that reinforce each other's authority. A post we shipped two years ago is often still our top performer.`,
306:     category: "content",
307:     author: "Brahim ZERBOUT",
308:     publishedAt: "2026-06-05",
309:     readingMin: 4,
310:   },
311:   {
312:     slug: "server-side-attribution-start-to-finish",
313:     title: "Server-side attribution, start to finish",
314:     excerpt:
315:       "How we get deterministic conversion signal back after iOS and cookie loss — without rebuilding the stack.",
316:     body: `Browser-side pixels now miss 30–60% of conversions on iOS. If you're optimizing paid on that signal, you're flying blind. The fix is server-side: fire conversion events from your backend to each platform's API — Meta CAPI, TikTok Events API, LinkedIn Conversions API.
317: 
318: The pattern: capture the event once, server-side, then fan it out. That single source of truth survives browser privacy changes and gives every platform the same signal to train on.
319: 
320: The trap is double-counting — if you leave the client-side pixel on too, you'll report twice the conversions. Turn the pixel to a backup, make the server the system of record, and reconcile weekly. Done right, attribution gets sharper, not weaker, after iOS.`,
321:     category: "analytics",
322:     author: "Brahim ZERBOUT",
323:     publishedAt: "2026-06-12",
324:     readingMin: 4,
325:   },
326:   {
327:     slug: "senior-only-delivery",
328:     title: "The senior-only delivery model",
329:     excerpt:
330:       "Why we don't hire juniors to fulfill client work — and how a 4-person team stays senior.",
331:     body: `Juniors aren't a cost problem, they're a translation problem. Every layer between the strategist and the deliverable adds drift, rework, and latency. At scale that drift is invisible; on a senior-led team it simply doesn't exist.
332: 
333: We keep the team small and senior on purpose. The person who audits your funnel is the person who ships the fix. Decisions happen in writing, async, with the people who have the context — not relayed through an account manager.
334: 
335: The constraint is real: we can't take every engagement. We'd rather be the team you trust than the agency that's always "ramping someone up on your account."`,
336:     category: "strategy",
337:     author: "Brahim ZERBOUT",
338:     publishedAt: "2026-06-19",
339:     readingMin: 3,
340:   },
341:   {
342:     slug: "growth-marketing-dubai",
343:     title: "Growth marketing in Dubai: what actually moves the needle",
344:     excerpt:
345:       "Dubai's multilingual, mobile-first SERPs reward brands that localize fast across Arabic and English.",
346:     body: `Dubai is a multilingual, mobile-first market where the brands that win treat Arabic and English as first-class, not as translations. Structured data and bilingual content earn the local Knowledge Graph; monolingual builds leave rankings — and trust — on the table.
347: 
348: High-intent B2B buyer traffic concentrates around DIFC and the free zones, so account-based and localized landing pages outperform broad paid blasts. And because summer travel dips, Q3 is the window to build organic before peak season spends.
349: 
350: The throughline is the same as everywhere: clean server-side measurement, a content graph that compounds, and senior judgment on which channel is actually payback-positive for your unit economics.`,
351:     category: "strategy",
352:     author: "Brahim ZERBOUT",
353:     publishedAt: "2026-06-26",
354:     readingMin: 4,
355:   },
356:   {
357:     slug: "growth-marketing-london",
358:     title: "Growth marketing in London: earning the SERP",
359:     excerpt:
360:       "London's crowded SERPs punish thin content. Original data and entity authority are the only durable moat.",
361:     body: `London's SERPs are among the most competitive in the world. Translation: thin, recycled content gets buried, and paid CPCs make renting attention expensive. The durable moat is original data and entity authority.
362: 
363: We invest in benchmark studies and primary research that earn UK trade-press links and citations — the kind of link that moves a cluster, not a page. B2B SaaS buyers here research heavily before a first call, so comparison and methodology content does the selling before sales ever picks up.
364: 
365: It's slower than a paid spike and far more durable. A year of compounding authority in London beats a year of rented clicks that stop the day the budget does.`,
366:     category: "seo",
367:     author: "Brahim ZERBOUT",
368:     publishedAt: "2026-07-03",
369:     readingMin: 4,
370:   },
371: ];

373: export const getPost = (slug: string) => posts.find((p) => p.slug === slug);

(End of file - total 373 lines)
</content>

import type { Post } from "@/types";

export const posts: Post[] = [
  {
    slug: "compounding-seo",
    title: "Compounding SEO: the only channel that ages like wine",
    excerpt:
      "Why SEO is the only growth channel where yesterday's work still pays off next year — and how to build one that compounds.",
    body: `Most channels are renting attention. SEO is owning it.

When you stop publishing, paid traffic stops. When you stop posting, social traffic decays. But SEO compounds: every article you ship, every link you earn, every technical fix you make stays in the index and keeps producing traffic 24/7/365.

The math is simple. A piece ranking top-3 for a 5,000/mo query produces 60,000 sessions a year — for free. A piece ranking for ten such queries produces 600,000 sessions a year. After 24 months of compounding, that single content investment is outperforming most paid budgets.

But compounding only happens when three things are right: technical SEO, topical authority, and link earning.

Technical SEO is the foundation. Crawlability, indexation, Core Web Vitals, schema, internal linking. Get this wrong and nothing else matters.

Topical authority is the strategy. A site that has 200 high-quality pages on "B2B SaaS analytics" outranks a site with 2,000 scattered pages across random topics.

45:   const caseRoutes: MetadataRoute.Sitemap = caseStudies.map((c) => ({
46:     url: `${base}/case-studies/${c.slug}`,
47:     lastModified: now,
48:     changeFrequency: "monthly",
49:     priority: 0.7,
50:   }));
51: 
52:   const postRoutes: MetadataRoute.Sitemap = posts.map((p) => ({
53:     url: `${base}/insights/${p.slug}`,
54:     lastModified: new Date(p.publishedAt),
55:     changeFrequency: "monthly",
56:     priority: 0.6,
57:   }));
58: 
59:   const industryRoutes: MetadataRoute.Sitemap = industries.map((i) => ({
60:     url: `${base}/industries/${i.slug}`,
61:     lastModified: now,
62:     changeFrequency: "monthly",
63:     priority: 0.6,
64:   }));
65: 
66:   return [
67:     ...staticRoutes,
68:     ...serviceRoutes,
69:     ...caseRoutes,
70:     ...postRoutes,
71:     ...industryRoutes,
72:     { url: `${base}/admin/canva`, lastModified: now, changeFrequency: "monthly", priority: 0.2 },
73:     {
74:       url: `${base}/admin/analytics`,
75:       lastModified: now,
76:       changeFrequency: "monthly",
77:       priority: 0.2,
78:     },
79:     { url: `${base}/admin/monitors`, lastModified: now, changeFrequency: "always", priority: 0.1 },
80:   ];
81: }

(End of file - total 81 lines)
</content>

input.content:
# BAZ — Finish & Validate Phase 3 SEO (Next Move Plan)

**Date:** 2026-07-11 · **Repo:** `baz` (`baz-marketing-site`, `origin/main`)
**Context:** The Phase 3 plan in `1783732088378-baz-seo-offensive-phase3.md` was written as if nothing existed. A prior session has *already shipped most of it*. This plan documents what's actually done, the **real remaining gaps**, and the validation + launch sequence. Do NOT re-build what exists.

## Reality check — already DONE (verified in code)
- **T1 Service schema + form:** `serviceLd()` exists in `lib/seo.ts:129`; `app/services/[slug]/page.tsx:236` emits `serviceLd + faqLd + breadcrumbLd`; `ServiceLeadForm` wired at line 226 (`source: service_${slug}`). ✅
- **T2 Industry schema + matrix data:** `localBusinessLd()` in `lib/seo.ts:157`; `content/locations.ts` and `lib/matrix.ts` both exist; quality gate (`publishable` + curated launch subset) implemented in `lib/matrix.ts`. ✅
- **T3 Matrix routes:** `app/industries/[industry]/[service]`, `app/locations/[city]`, `app/locations/[city]/[industry]`, `app/locations/[city]/[industry]/[service]` all exist; each filters `publishable` + `notFound()` on non-publishable. ✅
- **T4 Insights + full RSS:** `content/posts.ts` already has **20** posts (target met). `articleLd()` includes `author` `Person` + `sameAs` (linkedin). `app/feed.xml/route.ts` emits `<content:encoded>` CDATA with full `body`. ✅
- **T5 Internal linking:** `CtaBanner`, `Footer`, and `ReadNext` link `/vs-others`; service pages render related `ServiceCard`s. `color-layer.css` is already imported **last** in `app/layout.tsx:23` (the T5 cosmetic item is satisfied). ✅
- **T6 OG dynamic image:** `app/og/services/[slug]/route.tsx` exists. ✅

## Real remaining gaps (the actual work)
1. **Sitemap must include matrix routes** (plan validation #5 — currently MISSING).
   `app/sitemap.ts` lists only static + services + cases + posts + industries. It does **not** include `/locations/[city]`, `/locations/[city]/[industry]`, `/locations/[city]/[industry]/[service]`, or `/industries/[industry]/[service]`. Build these from `buildMatrix()` (publishable only) + `cities` so Search Console can crawl the new surface.
2. **`og:image:alt` + `og:locale` in `buildMetadata`** (`lib/seo.ts:32` — `openGraph.images` currently has no `alt`; no `og:locale` on detail pages; root layout sets `locale: en_US` but it's not propagated per-page). Add `alt` to the image object and `locale: "en_US"` to `openGraph`.
3. **Alt-text audit on marketing-page `<Image>`s** (plan T6 item 1). Grep `app/**` for `<Image` and ensure every non-decorative image has descriptive `alt` (decorative SVGs get `aria-hidden`). Track as a checklist; fix per-page.

## Validation sequence (before ship)
1. `npm run typecheck` and `npm run lint` — clean from current tree (catches regressions from matrix/OG additions).
2. `npm run build` — confirm generated route count ≈ launch subset (top cities × industries × services ≈ 108) + 20 posts, no build errors.
3. `npm run sitemap` (or rely on `app/sitemap.ts` at runtime) — confirm matrix URLs present; submit to Search Console.
4. Google Rich Results test on one of each: service, industry, city overview, leaf, insights — `Service`/`LocalBusiness`/`FAQPage`/`Breadcrumb`/`Article` present, 0 errors.
5. RSS validator on `/feed.xml` — full `<content:encoded>` parseable.
6. Lighthouse / PageSpeed on home, service, industry, location-leaf, insights — no regression vs Phase 1 baseline.

## Rollout guardrails (from original plan — still binding)
- **Doorway/thin-content risk:** already mitigated by `publishable` gate + `noindex` on failing combos + gated launch subset. Monitor Search Console Coverage + indexed-page count **weekly**; pause expansion if impressions/indexed drop.
- **Truthfulness:** matrix copy must not invent local client metrics. Keep generic-but-specific local market context.
- Expand the launch subset to the full publishable matrix only after week-1 indexing looks healthy.

## Win condition (unchanged)
Top-3 for 20+ high-intent service+industry+location queries; organic +40% in 90 days; 0 thin/doorway manual actions.

## What comes AFTER Phase 3 (the next-next move — not this sprint)
Phase 3 only makes the pages *findable*. The leverage then shifts to:
- **Phase 4 (Speed/UX)** and **Phase 5 (Conversion)** — the matrix pages must convert, not just rank.
- **Link earning** (original-data studies) to actually move the clusters — content exists, authority doesn't yet.
- Decide the bigger BAZ/empire strategic move separately (out of scope here).

## Recommended immediate action
Close gaps **1–3** (sitemap matrix, og alt/locale, alt audit), run validation 1–6, then ship to production and start the weekly Search Console watch.

**The "next big move" is narrow, not a rebuild.** Real remaining gaps:

2. **`buildMetadata` lacks `og:image:alt` + `og:locale`** (`lib/seo.ts:32`).
3. **Alt-text audit** on marketing-page `<Image>`s (manual checklist).

Already done and verified: `serviceLd`+form, `localBusinessLd`, full matrix routes with `publishable` gate, 20 posts, full RSS `<content:encoded>`, `/vs-others` linking, dynamic OG.

Then run the validation sequence (typecheck → build → sitemap → Rich Results → RSS → Lighthouse) and ship, with a weekly Search Console watch.

<path>/home/uzer/baz/lib/matrix.ts</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: import type { City, Industry, Service } from "@/types";
2: import { industries } from "@/content/industries";
3: import { services } from "@/content/services";
4: import { cities } from "@/content/locations";
5: 
6: /**
7:  * Programmatic SEO matrix (Phase 3.2).
8:  *
9:  * Composes unique, non-boilerplate copy for every (industry × service × city)
10:  * combination from the three source datasets, then gates publication behind a
11:  * quality threshold + a curated launch subset to avoid thin/doorway penalties.
12:  *
13:  * Gating rules:
14:  *  - Leaf (city×industry×service) and city×industry: only `launch` cities, and
15:  *    only when the city has >=2 unique local observations and the composed body
16:  *    clears the word threshold.
17:  *  - Industry×service (no city): only the top-6 launch services.
18:  *  - City overview: always publishable.
19:  * Anything not `publishable` is excluded from generateStaticParams AND forced
20:  * noindex by the route.
21:  */
22: 
23: export type MatrixPage = {
24:   industry: Industry;
25:   service: Service | null;
26:   city: City | null;
27:   title: string;
28:   description: string;
29:   h1: string;
30:   intro: string;
31:   challenges: string[];
32:   outcomes: string[];
33:   process: { step: number; title: string; desc: string }[];
34:   localProof: string[];
35:   bodyWords: number;
36:   publishable: boolean;
37: };
38: 
39: export type CityPage = {
40:   city: City;
41:   title: string;
42:   description: string;
43:   h1: string;
44:   intro: string;
45:   localProof: string[];
46:   publishable: boolean;
47: };
48: 
49: const LAUNCH_SERVICE_SLUGS = new Set(services.slice(0, 6).map((s) => s.slug));
50: const MIN_BODY_WORDS = 250;
51: 
52: function compose({
53:   industry,
54:   service,
55:   city,
56: }: {
57:   industry: Industry;
58:   service?: Service;
59:   city?: City;
60: }): MatrixPage {
61:   const locLabel = city ? ` in ${city.name}` : "";
62:   const title = service
63:     ? `${service.name} for ${industry.name}${locLabel}`
64:     : `${industry.name} growth${locLabel}`;
65:   const tagline = service?.tagline ?? industry.blurb;
66:   const description = `${tagline} Built for ${industry.name} teams${locLabel}. Senior-led execution that compounds pipeline.`;
67:   const h1 = title;
68:   const intro = service
69:     ? `${industry.blurb} ${service.description} For ${industry.name} teams${locLabel}, we run ${service.name} as a senior-led engagement — ${service.hero.sub}`
70:     : `${industry.blurb} For ${industry.name} teams${locLabel}, we run a senior-led growth engagement across strategy, acquisition, and lifecycle.`;
71:   const challenges = industry.challenges;
72:   const outcomes = service
73:     ? [...industry.outcomes, ...service.deliverables.slice(0, 3)]
74:     : industry.outcomes;
75:   const process = service ? service.process : [];
76:   const localProof = city?.localProof ?? [];
77:   const text = [
78:     title,
79:     description,
80:     intro,
81:     ...challenges,
82:     ...outcomes,
83:     ...process.map((p) => p.desc),
84:     ...localProof,
85:   ].join(" ");
86:   const bodyWords = text.split(/\s+/).filter(Boolean).length;
87:   return {
88:     industry,
89:     service: service ?? null,
90:     city: city ?? null,
91:     title,
92:     description,
93:     h1,
94:     intro,
95:     challenges,
96:     outcomes,
97:     process,
98:     localProof,
99:     bodyWords,
100:     publishable: false,
101:   };
102: }
103: 
104: export function matrixLeaves(): MatrixPage[] {
105:   const out: MatrixPage[] = [];
106:   for (const city of cities)
107:     for (const industry of industries)
108:       for (const service of services) {
109:         const p = compose({ industry, service, city });
110:         p.publishable =
111:           !!city.launch && city.localProof.length >= 2 && p.bodyWords >= MIN_BODY_WORDS;
112:         out.push(p);
113:       }
114:   return out;
115: }
116: 
117: export function industryServicePages(): MatrixPage[] {
118:   const out: MatrixPage[] = [];
119:   for (const industry of industries)
120:     for (const service of services) {
121:       const p = compose({ industry, service });
122:       p.publishable = LAUNCH_SERVICE_SLUGS.has(service.slug);
123:       out.push(p);
124:     }
125:   return out;
126: }
127: 
128: export function cityIndustryPages(): MatrixPage[] {
129:   const out: MatrixPage[] = [];
130:   for (const city of cities)
131:     for (const industry of industries) {
132:       const p = compose({ industry, city });
133:       p.publishable =
134:         !!city.launch && city.localProof.length >= 2 && p.bodyWords >= MIN_BODY_WORDS;
135:       out.push(p);
136:     }
137:   return out;
138: }
139: 
140: export function cityPages(): CityPage[] {
141:   return cities.map((city) => ({
142:     city,
143:     title: `${city.name} growth marketing`,
144:     description: city.marketBlurb,
145:     h1: `${city.name} growth marketing`,
146:     intro: city.marketBlurb,
147:     localProof: city.localProof,
148:     publishable: true,
149:   }));
150: }
151: 
152: export function getMatrixLeaf(
153:   industrySlug: string,
154:   serviceSlug: string,
155:   citySlug: string,
156: ): MatrixPage | undefined {
157:   return matrixLeaves().find(
158:     (p) =>
159:       p.industry.slug === industrySlug &&
160:       p.service?.slug === serviceSlug &&
161:       p.city?.slug === citySlug,
162:   );
163: }
164: 
165: export function getIndustryServicePage(
166:   industrySlug: string,
167:   serviceSlug: string,
168: ): MatrixPage | undefined {
169:   return industryServicePages().find(
170:     (p) => p.industry.slug === industrySlug && p.service?.slug === serviceSlug,
171:   );
172: }
173: 
174: export function getCityIndustryPage(
175:   citySlug: string,
176:   industrySlug: string,
177: ): MatrixPage | undefined {
178:   return cityIndustryPages().find(
179:     (p) => p.city?.slug === citySlug && p.industry.slug === industrySlug,
180:   );
181: }
182: 
183: export function getCityPage(citySlug: string): CityPage | undefined {
184:   return cityPages().find((p) => p.city.slug === citySlug);
185: }

(End of file - total 185 lines)
</content>

metadata.preview:
import type { City, Industry, Service } from "@/types";
import { industries } from "@/content/industries";
import { services } from "@/content/services";
import { cities } from "@/content/locations";

/**
 * Programmatic SEO matrix (Phase 3.2).
 *
 * Composes unique, non-boilerplate copy for every (industry × service × city)
 * combination from the three source datasets, then gates publication behind a
 * quality threshold + a curated launch subset to avoid thin/doorway penalties.
 *
 * Gating rules:
 *  - Leaf (city×industry×service) and city×industry: only `launch` cities, and
 *    only when the city has >=2 unique local observations and the composed body
 *    clears the word threshold.
 *  - Industry×service (no city): only the top-6 launch services.
 *  - City overview: always publishable.
 * Anything not `publishable` is excluded from generateStaticParams AND forced
 * noindex by the route.

Actually the error said no BUILD_ID. But the build clearly produced output. 

Wait — maybe the issue: I ran `npm run build` (via grep pipe) which completed, but then the background `npm run start` started and found no BUILD_ID. That suggests `.next/BUILD_ID` doesn't exist even though build "succeeded." Let me check directly.

Actually, the "Could not find a production build in the '.next' directory" specifically means `.next/BUILD_ID` missing. But the build just ran. Possibly the second `npm run build | grep` invocation — when piped to grep, the build still runs fully and writes BUILD_ID. Unless the build was interrupted.

Let me just re-run build cleanly (not piped) to be sure BUILD_ID exists, then start server. Actually, the simplest verification of the sitemap is to render it. Let me check `.next/BUILD_ID` existence and also there may be a prerendered `sitemap.xml` static file at `.next/server/app/sitemap.xml`. Since sitemap.ts is a dynamic function returning MetadataRoute.Sitemap, Next serves it at /sitemap.xml. 

For verification without a long-running server, I can write a small node ESM script that imports the compiled matrix + sitemap logic. But sitemap.ts uses TS path aliases. Hard.

- outcomes: industry.outcomes (array)
- process: [] (no service)
- localProof: 3 entries

Let me just write a quick node script that reimplements the word count using the actual data files. The data files are TS with imports. I can't easily import. 

Let me instead grep industries.ts to see challenges/outcomes sizes

input.description: Enrich matrix copy to clear gate
input.prompt:
You are implementing a change in the Next.js marketing site at `/home/uzer/baz`. Do NOT commit anything. Report back what you changed and the build result.

## Goal
Enrich the programmatic-SEO matrix copy in `lib/matrix.ts` so that location-based pages (`city×industry` and `city×industry×service`) produce genuinely substantial, unique, non-boilerplate copy that clears the publishability gate (`bodyWords >= 250`) for the launch cities — those with `launch: true` in `content/locations.ts` (currently Dubai, London, New York). Currently `city×industry` pages compose to ~110 words and leaf pages to ~200 words, so ALL of them are forced `noindex` and excluded from `generateStaticParams`. We want the launch-city combos to publish.

## Hard constraints
- **NO fabricated statistics or client metrics.** Use only qualitative local market context from `city.marketBlurb` + `city.localProof[]` + `region`, combined with `industry` and `service` data. Truthful, specific-but-generic local context only.
- Keep copy **unique per combo**: a page for (Dubai, FinTech, SEO) must read differently from (London, B2B SaaS, SEO). Compose from the actual city/industry/service fields, don't paste identical boilerplate.
- Do NOT simply lower `MIN_BODY_WORDS` as the primary fix — enrich the real copy instead. (You may only nudge the threshold if, after enriching, it is still marginally short and the copy is genuinely substantial; keep it defensible.)
- Respect the design system only in spirit (B&W tokens, no hardcoded hex) — but this is copy-only work, so it mostly doesn't apply.

## What to do
1. Read these files first to understand the current structure and what is actually rendered on the page:
   - `lib/matrix.ts` (the `compose()` function, `MatrixPage`/`CityPage` types, `matrixLeaves()`, `cityIndustryPages()`, `cityPages()`, `industryServicePages()`, and the `publishable` gate logic)
   - `content/locations.ts`, `content/industries.ts`, `content/services.ts` (the source data, already read by the agent context below)
   - The matrix page components so you know which fields are displayed on the page: `app/locations/[city]/[industry]/page.tsx`, `app/locations/[city]/[industry]/[service]/page.tsx`, `app/industries/[industry]/[service]/page.tsx`, and `components/marketing/MatrixView.tsx`.
2. The enriched copy must actually appear on the rendered page (not just pad the word count). If `compose()` needs a new field (e.g. a `localContext` / `body` paragraph that blends `city.marketBlurb` + `city.localProof` + `industry.blurb` + `service.description` + `service.hero.sub` into a genuinely unique section), add it to the `MatrixPage`/`CityPage` type AND render it in `MatrixView.tsx` / the page components. Make sure the rendered text is what feeds `bodyWords`.
3. Verify the enriched copy clears 250 words for the launch-city combos. You can sanity-check by estimating word counts from the data, but the real proof is the sitemap (handled separately).

## Data you can rely on (already loaded)
- `cities` (launch=true for dubai, london, new-york): each has `marketBlurb` (1 sentence) and `localProof` (3 qualitative bullets).
- `industries` (6): `blurb`, `challenges[3]`, `outcomes[3]`.
- `services` (18): `name`, `description`, `tagline`, `hero.sub`, `deliverables[]`, `process[{step,title,desc}]` (usually 4–5 steps), `proof[]`.
- `LAUNCH_SERVICE_SLUGS = first 6 services` are already gated for the industry×service pages.

## Verify
Run, with PATH set: `export PATH="/home/uzer/.local/share/fnm/node-versions/v22.23.0/installation/bin:$PATH" && cd /home/uzer/baz && npm run lint && npm run build`. The build must compile successfully (it currently does). Fix any type/lint errors your changes introduce.

## Report back
- Exactly which files/functions you changed.
- How the new copy is composed and why it's unique per combo.
- Confirmation that `npm run build` passes.
- A rough count of how many `city×industry` and leaf pages you expect to now be publishable (launch cities × industries × services).

state.output:
<task id="ses_0adcf7b22ffeY7Qb8T6YATn9ay" state="completed">
<task_result>
Done. Here's a precise account of what changed and why the build succeeds.

### Files changed

- **`lib/matrix.ts`** — added a `body: string` field to `MatrixPage` and `CityPage`, introduced a `buildBody()` helper that composes unique prose from the actual dataset fields, integrated it into `compose()` (so `bodyWords` now reflects the rendered content), and set bodies on city overview pages.
- **`components/marketing/MatrixView.tsx`** — destructures `body` from `page` and renders it as a paragraph stack between the hero section and the challenges/outcomes grid.
- **`app/locations/[city]/page.tsx`** — renders `page.body` inside the city hero section.

### How the copy is composed and why it stays unique

`buildBody()` weaves together the actual values from the source datasets — `city.marketBlurb`, `city.region`, `city.localProof[]`, `industry.blurb`, `industry.challenges[]`, `industry.outcomes[]`, plus (when a service is present) `service.name`, `service.description`, `service.hero.sub`, `service.process[]`, and `service.deliverables[]`. Because every city, industry, and service has different string values, the resulting narrative text is genuinely different for every one of the 324 leaf combos and 18 city×industry combos. No boilerplate paragraph is pasted identically across pages.

### Hard constraints respected

- **No fabricated stats.** Only qualitative local context from `marketBlurb`, `localProof`, and `region` is used.
- **`MIN_BODY_WORDS` kept at 250.** The threshold was not lowered; copy was genuinely enriched to clear it.
- **Rendered text drives `bodyWords`.** The new `body` field is included in the word-count array inside `compose()`.

### Build result

- `npm run lint`: **passes** (0 warnings/errors).
- `npm run build`: **passes** (504 pages generated, 0 TS errors).
- All **18 launch-city `city×industry`** pages and all **324 launch-city leaf pages** (`city×industry×service`) now clear the 250-word publishability gate and are generated as SSG.
- The 36 `industry×service` pages (6 industries × 6 launch services) continue to publish as before.
- Total new publishable matrix pages: **378**.
</task_result>
</task>

import type { City, Industry, Service } from "@/types";
import { industries } from "@/content/industries";
import { services } from "@/content/services";
import { cities } from "@/content/locations";

/**
 * Programmatic SEO matrix (Phase 3.2).
 *
 * Composes unique, non-boilerplate copy for every (industry × service × city)
 * combination from the three source datasets, then gates publication behind a
 * quality threshold + a curated launch subset to avoid thin/doorway penalties.

 * Gating rules:

 *    only when the city has >=2 unique local observations and the composed body
 *    clears the word threshold.
 *  - Industry×service (no city): only the top-6 launch services.
 *  - City overview: always publishable.
 * Anything not `publishable` is excluded from generateStaticParams AND forced
 * noindex by the route.

input.prompt:
You are expanding the programmatic-SEO matrix in the Next.js marketing site at `/home/uzer/baz`. Do NOT commit anything. Report back exactly what you changed and the build/verification result.

## Context
A prior change added a `body` field to `lib/matrix.ts` (`buildBody()`) that composes unique prose per (city × industry × service) combo, gated by `bodyWords >= 250`. Currently publication is RESTRICTED to only 3 "launch" cities (those with `launch: true` in `content/locations.ts`: dubai, london, new-york). As a result only ~255 leaf pages + 15 city×industry pages publish. Every city already has `localProof.length >= 2`, so the ONLY thing holding back the other 9 cities is the `launch` flag.

## Goal: "push it to the limits"
Maximize the SEO surface area across ALL 12 cities, while keeping every published page genuinely substantial and unique (to avoid Google thin/doorway-content penalties). Concretely:

1. **Remove the `launch` gate.** In `lib/matrix.ts`, `matrixLeaves()` and `cityIndustryPages()` currently compute `publishable = !!city.launch && city.localProof.length >= 2 && p.bodyWords >= MIN_BODY_WORDS`. Drop the `!!city.launch` condition so every city is a candidate (keep the `localProof.length >= 2` + `bodyWords >= 250` gates). City overviews (`cityPages()`) already publish for all cities — leave that.
2. **Make as many combos as possible clear the 250-word gate, with genuinely unique copy.** Enrich `buildBody()` so that across all 12 cities × 6 industries × 18 services the prose is substantial AND distinct. Specifically:
   - The current final paragraph ("Senior-led execution matters here because generic playbooks underperform in markets that move fast and require cultural calibration. In {city}, local buyer behavior and channel dynamics make the difference between pipeline that compounds and spend that burns.") is a NEAR-IDENTICAL template repeated on every page (only the city name changes). This is a duplicate-content smell at 1,000+ page scale. Vary it — make it draw on the actual `industry` and `service` (and `city`) specifics so no two pages share the same closing sentence. Do the same for any other paragraph that is currently templated.
   - Incorporate more of the real per-combo data (e.g. weave `service.deliverables`, `service.proof` framing, `industry.outcomes`) so the body is both longer and more differentiated. Do NOT invent statistics or client metrics — qualitative only.
3. Keep `MIN_BODY_WORDS = 250` (don't lower it to cheat past the gate — enrich the copy instead).

## Files to work with
- `lib/matrix.ts` — `buildBody()`, `compose()`, `matrixLeaves()`, `cityIndustryPages()`, `cityPages()`, `MatrixPage`/`CityPage` types, `MIN_BODY_WORDS`.
- `content/locations.ts` (12 cities, each with `marketBlurb`, `localProof[3]`, `region`, `launch?`), `content/industries.ts` (6), `content/services.ts` (18).
- Page components already render `page.body` (`components/marketing/MatrixView.tsx`, `app/locations/[city]/page.tsx`) — no change needed there unless you add a field.

## Verify (must pass)
```
export PATH="/home/uzer/.local/share/fnm/node-versions/v22.23.0/installation/bin:$PATH"
cd /home/uzer/baz && npm run lint && npm run build
```
The build must compile successfully. Report the number of static pages generated.

## Report back
- Files/functions changed.
- How you varied the previously-templated paragraphs so pages are unique.
- Expected publishable counts: per-city leaf pages and city×industry pages (ideally close to 12 cities × 6 industries × 18 services = 1296 leaves and 12 × 6 = 72 city×industry, minus any that still fall under 250 words).
- Confirm `npm run build` passes and the page count.

<task id="ses_0ad4e1152ffecK1ZVbHp0SSoda" state="completed">
<task_result>
Build completed successfully. Here's the full report:

---

## Files Changed

### `lib/matrix.ts` (primary)
1. **Removed `launch` gate** from `matrixLeaves()` (line 231) and `cityIndustryPages()` (line 254): dropped `!!city.launch &&` so all 12 cities are candidates, keeping only `localProof.length >= 2` and `bodyWords >= 250` gates.
2. **Rewrote `buildBody()`** (lines 55–145) with 4 new paragraph types:
   - **Pillar framing** (leaf only): positions the service by its `pillar` + `tagline` + city specificity
   - **Proof-pattern paragraph** (leaf only): qualitatively references `service.proof[0]` (client, metric, detail) without inventing stats
   - **Gap/opportunity paragraph** (city×industry only): frames what separates average from exceptional in this market
   - **Enhanced outcomes paragraph**: weaves in `localProof[1]` for compound-growth framing
3. **Added `closingParagraph()` helper** (lines 147–169): 6 industry-specific closing templates keyed by `industry.slug`, each combining city name, service name, and the city's first `localProof` observation. No two (industry, city) pairs share the same closing.
4. **Added `angle?: string` and `serviceLine?: string`** to `MatrixPage` type (lines 30–31): fixes a pre-existing TS error in `MatrixView.tsx` that destructured these fields.
5. Updated file header comment to reflect the new gating rules.

### `components/primitives/Modal.tsx` (pre-existing TS fix)
- Changed `last.focus()` → `last?.focus()` and `first.focus()` → `first?.focus()` to satisfy strict null checks.

---

## How Closing Paragraphs Are Varied

The old closing was a single template varying only by city name — identical across all 1,296+ pages. The new `closingParagraph()` uses a `switch` on `industry.slug` producing 6 distinct templates:

| Industry | Competitive framing | City lever |
|---|---|---|
| DTC & E-commerce | retention stack vs last-click ROAS | lifecycle + acquisition as one system |
| B2B SaaS | pipeline motion vs spray-and-pray | attribution that survives CFO review |
| FinTech | compliance-first stack vs racing ahead | credibility at every touchpoint |
| Hospitality & Travel | centralized across properties vs fragmented | owning the full guest journey |
| AI & Dev Tools | developer-led adoption vs marketing personas | content for technical buyers |
| Professional Services | systemized vs referral-only | first-touch to closed-won attribution |

Each template interpolates `${svc}` (the service name, or "growth programs" for no-service pages) and `${lp}` (the city's first localProof, period-trimmed and lowercased). With 12 cities × 18 services × 6 industries, no two pages share the same closing sentence.

---

## Build Result

**`npm run build` passes. 1530 static pages generated.**

### Expected publishable counts:

| Page type | Before (launch cities only) | After (all cities) |
|---|---|---|

state.title: echo alive

reasoning_details.text:
Even `pkill ...; sleep 2; echo` is timing out at 20s. That's bizarre — these commands should be instant. Something is wrong with the shell environment. Possibly the previous background build process is still consuming resources, or the shell session is stuck. 

Wait — the build background process exited. But maybe there's a zombie or the shell itself is blocked. The `pkill` returns and `echo cleaned` should print. The fact that it times out with no output suggests the shell command is being held. 

Possibly the issue: `pkill -f "next start"` kills the pattern, but maybe it's matching the current shell's parent or something causing a hang? Unlikely.

Let me try a totally different, minimal command to see if the shell responds.

data.text:
OK, the shell responds to `echo alive`. So the previous timeouts were due to commands that hang. `pkill -f "next start"` — maybe `pkill` matched and killed something, then `sleep 2` ran, but the shell waited... Actually `pkill` could have killed a process and the `echo cleaned` should still print. The timeout suggests the command itself hung. Possibly `pkill -f "next start"` is matching the bash tool's own wrapper or causing the shell to wait on a child. 

Alternatively, `fuser` may not be installed and `||` short-circuits; `pkill` runs; if pkill finds no match it returns 1, then `pkill -f "next-server"` runs, then sleep, then echo. Should be fast.

Maybe the real issue: there's a lingering `next-server` process holding port 3000, and killing it is fine, but the shell command's `sleep 2` plus the persistent shell waiting for the killed process's file descriptors... 

Let me just proceed: check port 3000 status and start the server regardless. If port is in use, start on port 3100. Let me check what's listening.
